title: Satellite Systems Engineer
setting: Satellite manufacturer (e.g., Airbus Defence & Space or a NewSpace startup)
description: You are a satellite systems engineer working on a new Earth observation constellation. Your role spans the entire satellite — from power system sizing to thermal design to communications budgets. You are the person who ensures all the subsystems work together as an integrated system. With the rise of mega-constellations, the industry is shifting from bespoke, one-off satellites to mass-produced platforms — and you are at the centre of that transition.

schedule.activity: Start with a systems health check. Review telemetry from the three satellites already in orbit. One satellite's solar array is generating 3% less power than predicted — flag for investigation.
schedule.note: Satellite engineers often support operations for hardware already in orbit while designing the next generation.

schedule.activity: Systems engineering meeting. Walk through the mass budget for the next-generation satellite. The payload team wants 5 kg more for a higher-resolution camera. You need to find 5 kg elsewhere.
Work on the power budget. The new camera needs 80W more power. Run the analysis: can the solar array and battery support this through the worst-case eclipse? If not, size a larger array.
schedule.note: Everything in satellite design is interconnected — more power means bigger arrays, which means more mass, which affects structural design.

schedule.activity: Lunch. Chat with the constellation operations team about a conjunction warning — a piece of debris is predicted to pass within 500 metres of one of your satellites.
Link budget analysis for the data downlink. The new camera generates 2x more data. Can the existing X-band transmitter handle it, or do you need to upgrade to Ka-band?

Review a thermal analysis from a junior engineer. Check that the battery stays within its -5 to +45 degree Celsius operating range throughout the orbit. The hot case is marginal — suggest adding a radiator.
Interface control meeting with the launch vehicle provider. Your satellite must fit within the Falcon 9 fairing and survive the vibration environment. Review the ICD (Interface Control Document).
Update the system requirements document to capture today's decisions. Traceability from stakeholder needs to component specs must be maintained.
Head home. The pace is busy but more sustainable than launch vehicle work.

responsibilities: Manage satellite system-level budgets (mass, power, data, thermal)
Ensure subsystem interfaces are compatible and well-defined
Lead system design reviews and trade studies
Support satellite integration, testing, and launch campaigns
Monitor on-orbit performance and anomaly resolution
Flow requirements from mission level to subsystem level
challenges: Everything is interconnected — a change in one subsystem cascades everywhere
Mass and power margins are always tight — every gram and watt is contested
Testing a satellite in orbit-like conditions (thermal vacuum, vibration) is expensive and time-consuming
Schedule pressure from constellation deployment timelines
Balancing cost, performance, and reliability in a competitive commercial market
rewards: Seeing your satellite launch and send back its first images from orbit
Understanding the complete system — no other engineering role is as broadly integrated
Growing commercial satellite market with strong job demand
Working on technology that directly benefits life on Earth (Earth observation, communications)
Opportunity to work internationally — satellite companies are global
